prompt: Search hard for ANY product launched or announced in 2026 that does kolm's EXACT wedge: a cryptographically SIGNED 'AI agent security readiness' or 'agent attestation' report, mapped to SOC2/ISO 42001/NIST AI RMF/EU AI Act/OWASP/MITRE ATLAS, sold as a fixed fee, optionally with a named human co-signer. Name every direct or near-direct clone you can find (beyond Cyphrex), with launch date, pricing, and @handle/URL. If a true clone exists, quantify how close it is. If none exists, state that explicitly and characterize how defensible the gap is. Cite everything.
content:
**No products launched or announced in 2026 match Kolm’s (or Cyphrex’s) exact wedge.**[[1]](https://cyphrex.io/legal/privacy)[[2]](https://cyphrex.io/news/88-percent-companies-agent-security-incidents)

Cyphrex (cyphrex.io) positions itself in AI agent security/identity infrastructure, with content on regulatory compliance (e.g., EU AI Act deadlines), trust gaps, and governance for agentic AI. No public details in search results describe an exact fixed-fee, cryptographically signed “AI agent security readiness” or “agent attestation” report product explicitly mapped to SOC2/ISO 42001/NIST AI RMF/EU AI Act/OWASP/MITRE ATLAS, with an optional named human co-signer.[[3]](https://cyphrex.io/news/agentic-ai-regulatory-compliance-deadline-71-days)[[4]](https://cyphrex.io/news/trust-gap)

Extensive searches across web results for 2025–2026 (focusing on launches/announcements, “cryptographically signed,” “attestation report,” “security readiness,” fixed-fee/pricing, human co-signer, and the listed frameworks/standards) turned up:

- **Standards, drafts, and research papers** on attestation (e.g., IETF drafts for Entity Attestation Tokens/EAT profiles for AI agents, dynamic attestation protocols, differential attestation in systems like Omega). These are technical proposals or academic work, not commercial fixed-fee report products.[[5]](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-messous-eat-ai/00/)[[6]](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-jiang-seat-dynamic-attestation-00.html)
- **Hardware/rooted cryptographic attestation** (e.g., OPAQUE’s hardware-signed identity verification and “Attested Evidence Pack” using NVIDIA/Intel/AMD silicon; Fortanix AI attestation for TEEs; confidential computing remote attestation). These focus on runtime/hardware proofs or evidence packs, not multi-framework-mapped readiness reports sold as a fixed-fee service with optional human co-signing.[[7]](https://www.opaque.co/resources/articles/six-agencies-five-governments-one-message-your-ai-agents-cant-ignore)[[8]](https://www.fortanix.com/faq/ai-security/ai-attestation)
- **Checklists, frameworks, and governance tools** (e.g., CSA AI identity checklists, Okta readiness checklists, iternal.ai or NeuralTrust reports on agentic risks/OWASP/NIST/EU AI Act mapping, Nirmata AIBOM attestations via cosign/Sigstore for Kubernetes images, Kiteworks Compliant AI for data-layer governance). These are assessments, surveys, or policy-enforcement solutions, not signed commercial attestation reports.[[9]](https://cloudsecurityalliance.org/articles/ai-identity-security-compliance-checklist)[[10]](https://pages.okta.com/rs/855-QAH-699/images/OKTA1083_OKTA-ai-identity-readiness-checklist_v03.pdf?version=0)[[11]](https://iternal.ai/ai-agent-security-checklist)
- **Other concepts** like Agent Passports (verifiable credentials + capability attestation), Sigstore/in-toto provenance for agents/instructions, or notarization protocols (e.g., Sello receiver-attested receipts). None match the precise commercial model.[[12]](https://www.agentic-alliance.com/services)[[13]](https://arxiv.org/html/2606.04193v1)

No results describe a competing fixed-fee product with the exact combination of cryptographic signing of a readiness/attestation artifact, explicit multi-standard mapping (SOC2 + ISO 42001 + NIST AI RMF + EU AI Act + OWASP + MITRE ATLAS), and optional named human co-signer. Mentions of pricing are absent for any comparable offering; most are open-source, hardware/platform features, consulting/assurance services (e.g., PwC-style audits), or free/paid checklists/reports without the cryptographic or co-signer elements.[[14]](https://aminrj.com/posts/ai-security-threat-modeling-production/)

**No direct or near-direct clones exist beyond Cyphrex.** The gap appears defensible because it requires a specific commercial synthesis—cryptographic signing + broad compliance mapping in a report format + fixed-fee model + human co-signer—that no other vendor has replicated in public 2026 announcements or offerings. Related capabilities (hardware attestation, provenance signing, checklists) exist in pieces, but the integrated, signed, report-centric product does not. This leaves room for differentiation in a market still dominated by emerging standards, research prototypes, and partial solutions as of June 2026.
